citation_sentences: A 20 kg ingot of a Fe–10Mn–10Al–0.7C steel was prepared by vacuum induction melting method. The ingot was heated to $1 2 0 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ and then forged to forging blank with cross section of about 40 mm × 60 mm. After homogenized at $1 2 0 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ for 1 h, the forging blank was hot-rolled to the plate of 3 mm thickness, with the finishing rolling temperature around $8 8 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } _ { \scriptscriptstyle 1 }$ and then water quenched to about 300 °C. The hot-rolled plate was annealed at various temperatures ranging from $7 0 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ to $1 2 0 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ for the same time, 1 h, followed by water quenching. The hot rolling and annealing conditions are shown in Fig. 1.

The volume fraction change of each phase with the temperature, which is determined from the XRD measurement results, is shown in Fig. 14. The general trend is similar with that calculated by Thermo-Calc, which is shown in Fig. 1. No γ phase is detected when annealing temperature is $7 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } _ { \scriptscriptstyle 1 }$ and no κ-carbide is detected when the annealing temperature is $9 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathbb { C } .$ As has been obtained from the OM micrographs in Fig. 5, the dissolution temperature for κ-carbide is between $8 5 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ and $9 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathsf C ,$ which is higher than that $( 8 2 3 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } )$ calculated by Thermo-Calc (Fig. 1). When the anneal temperature changes from $7 0 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ to 900 °C, the volume fractions of α phase and κ-carbide decrease, and the fraction of γ phase increases. A $t 9 0 0 ^ { \circ } C ,$ the γ phase fraction is about 60%. After the dissolution of κ-carbide, the volume fraction of γ phase first increase slightly and then decrease slightly with the increment of annealing temperature, and the fraction of γ phase reaches largest around the annealing temperature of $1 0 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } .$ The volume fraction of γ phase changes only in the small range of 57% to 63% when the annealing temperature is between $9 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathsf { C }$ and $1 2 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } .$ . The boundary κ- carbide that was seen in Fig. 10 cannot be detected by XRD, probably due to that the fraction of boundary κ-carbide is too small. In addition, TEM analysis (not shown here) indicates that no obvious superlattice spots can be seen in the SADPs of α phase or γ phase in all the annealed specimens, and although some weak superlattice spots can be seen in the SADPs of some grains; they are all too weak to obtain the DF images. So ordering of atoms in α phase or γ phase is not expected during annealing at the temperature range $7 0 0 { - } 1 2 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ for 1 h, while ordering may occur during quenching, whereas the time is too short for the ordering to well develop.

citation_sentences: The phase diagram of the Fe–10Mn–10Al–0.7C steel was calculated by Thermo-Calc software with database TCFE7, and fractions of the equilibrium phases (ferrite (α), austenite (γ) and κ-carbide (κ)) when the temperature is over $5 5 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ are shown in Fig. 2. According to Fig. 2, ferrite is expected to form first during solidification, and then austenite forms by peritectic reaction [33]. After solidification, the phase fraction of ferrite will decrease and that of austenite will increase with the decrease of temperature until the temperature reaches around $1 0 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,$ where the fraction of ferrite is lowest. When the temperature is lower than about $1 0 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,$ the fraction of ferrite decreases with the decrease of temperature. However, the phase fractions do not change much until temperature becomes lower than $8 2 3 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,$ , where κ-carbide appears by eutectoid reaction $\gamma \alpha + \kappa .$ Apparently, a part of body-centered cubic (BCC) ferrite will always exist without transforming to facecentered (FCC) austenite, and this part of ferrite is often referred to as δ-ferrite [34,35]. The δ-ferrite usually exhibits coarse grains in the microstructure [13,34]. In the present study, δ-ferrite is not separately treated with α-ferrite. There is difference in the phase fractions and the eutectoid temperature for κ-carbide between the result calculated by Thermo-Calc and that measured from the steel, which will discussed later. The differences may come from that, the steel in the study is not in equilibrium state, and that the calculated fractions are based on the atomic fraction, while the measured fraction from XRD results is based on the volume fraction [34].

citation_sentences: The microstructure of hot rolled specimen (marked as “HR”) is shown in Fig. 3. Fig. 3(a) and (b) is OM micrographs of RD–ND (rolling direction–normal direction) plane, RD–TD (rolling direction–transverse direction) plane, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3(a), typical banded structure is presented, and coarse ferrite formed during the early stage of solidification steel is obviously observed. It is easy to see that the microstructure is very inhomogeneous in Fig. 3(b), and, large α phase area (the continuous area that mainly contains α phase, while there are also some γ phase or κ-carbide dispersed) and γ phase area (the continuous area that mainly contains γ phase, while there are also some α phase or κ-carbide dispersed) can be used to describe the microstructure. SEM micrograph in Fig. 3(c) clearly shows the κ-carbide along ferrite/austenite (α/γ) and ferrite/ferrite (α/α) boundaries. EDS is used to confirm the α phase, γ phase, and κ-carbide, since α phase is Al-rich, γ phase is Mn-rich and C-rich, while κ-carbide is Mn-rich and Al-rich [15,18,34].

citation_sentences: XRD results of hot rolled and annealed specimens are shown in Fig. 4. The peaks of κ-carbide are seen in specimen annealed at $7 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,$ 800 °C and $8 5 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,$ and no obvious peaks of γ phase can be seen in the specimen annealed at $7 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } .$ . In the hot rolled specimen, peaks of κ- carbide are not seen, probably due to the low volume fraction of κ- carbide, or the coherency of κ-carbide and α matrix [36]. Lattice parameters can be calculated from the XRD profiles, and results show that the lattice parameter of α phase changes in the range 2.89–2.91 Å, the lattice parameter of γ phase changes in the range 3.65–3.67 Å, and the lattice parameter of κ-carbide changes in the range 3.76–3.79 Å. The lattices of α phase and γ phase have been slightly expanded by Mn, Al and C atoms, and being larger than the lattices of α phase and γ phase in Fe–3.5Mn–5.8Al–0.35C, respectively [34]. It is said that the lattice of κ-carbide is mainly governed by the interstitial element, C, while the increment of Al would also bring expansion to the κ lattice and because Mn has a larger atomic radius than Fe, the substitute of Fe by Mn in κ- carbide would also cause slight expansion in the κ-carbide lattice [15, 34]. The lattice expansion of α phase and γ phase, which are the main phase constituents in the steel, will contribute to the low density of the steel.

Although no obvious κ-carbide is found distributed in the matrix when the annealing temperature is in the range $9 0 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ to $1 1 0 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ (Fig. 6(d) through (g)), κ-carbide is indeed found along some boundaries between large α grains in α area in the annealing temperature range $9 0 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ to $1 2 0 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ , as shown in $\mathrm { F i g . } 1 0 ( \mathsf { a } )$ through (d). TEM is used to confirm the κ-carbide along the α/α boundaries. Fig. 11(a) is the TEM BF micrograph of the specimen annealed at $1 1 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ , and the SADP of boundary κ-carbide and α matrix are shown in Fig. 11(b). Fig. 12(a) shows the TEM BF micrograph of the specimen annealed at $1 2 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ and the corresponding SADP in Fig. 12(b) shows the N–W orientation relationship between κ-carbide and α matrix. However, the (200)κ DF micrograph taken from $[ 1 1 0 ] _ { \kappa }$ direction $( \mathrm { F i g . } 1 2 ( \mathrm { c } ) )$ shows only part of the boundary κ-carbide, which indicates the large misorientation between the neighboring κ-carbide particles. The boundary κ- carbide is believed to form during the quenching when annealing temperature is over the dissolution temperature of κ-carbide, which is between $8 5 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ and $9 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathbb { C } .$ In the specimen annealed at $1 2 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,$ , there are needle shaped or plate shaped particles in some large α grains (Fig. 6(h) and Fig. 10(d)). The particle forms during the quenching, and basically has the FCC structure, and many investigations on the particles in the steels with a high Al content have been done by Hwang et al. [37–41]. EDS line scanning is done during SEM observation of the specimens annealed at $8 0 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ and $9 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathsf { C }$ to examine the elements concentrations in different phases, as shown in Fig. 13(a) and (b). In the specimen annealed at $8 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,$ Mn content is higher while Al content is lower in α phase compared with those in γ phase, and Mn and Al content in κ-carbide is the highest, higher than those in α phase or γ phase. The results are in accord with the normal conclusion well.

The γ phase has almost totally decomposed to α phase and κ- carbide by eutectoid reaction $\gamma \to \alpha + \kappa$ after annealing at $7 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ for 1 h, and the N–W orientation relationship is found mainly between some lamellar κ-carbide and the α matrix (Fig. 7). When annealed at $8 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ or $8 5 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,$ the γ phase volume fraction is about 29% and 39%, respectively. The κ-carbide and α phase fraction decreases, while the γ phase fraction increases with annealing temperature rising from 700 $^ \circ \mathsf { C }$ to $9 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } .$ The N–W orientation relationship has been hardly found between κ-carbide and α matrix when annealing temperature reaches $8 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } \left( \mathrm { F i g . } 8 \right)$ . In the annealing temperature range from 700 to 850 ${ } ^ { \circ } \mathbb { C } ,$ , the γ phase becomes more stable with the increase of temperature, so the γ phase fraction increases with the increase of temperature. The morphology of κ-carbide tends to be larger and closer to globular shape (Fig. 6(a) through (c)), which originates from the higher diffusion speed of elements and growth speed of κ-carbide at higher temperatures. For the sake of lowering the strain energy, the growth of κ- carbide favors to be along the plane of boundary between κ-carbide and α matrix with orientation relationship at first, and with the grow up of κ-carbide, the lattice coherency and orientation relationship will be destroyed. This is why the orientation relationship is easy to found between the lamellar κ and α matrix in the specimen annealed at 700 ${ } ^ { \circ } { \mathsf { C } } ,$ and becomes harder to be found or has disappeared when the annealing temperature rises from $7 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ to $8 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ .

When the annealing temperature rises from $9 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathsf { C }$ to $9 5 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } _ { \scriptscriptstyle { 1 } }$ , the α phase fraction decreases slightly (Fig. 14), and the movement of $\alpha / \gamma$ phase boundary toward α phase side has a more significant effect on the grain size of $\mathbf { \dot { \alpha } } _ { 1 } ,$ which is mainly distributed among γ grains and has a small initial grain size. From Fig. 6(d) and (e), which is the SEM micrograph of the specimen annealed at $9 0 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ and $1 0 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ , respectively, the size decrease of the $\alpha _ { 1 }$ grains among γ grains is evident with the annealing temperature rising from $9 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathsf { C }$ to $9 5 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ .

Yield phenomenon, which is usually seen in the ferrite steels [46], only appears in the specimen annealed at $7 0 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } .$ In the specimen annealed at $7 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,$ , the γ phase has almost totally transformed into α phase and κ-carbide, and the microstructure is composed of large α grains, continuous κ-carbide along α grain boundaries, and areas containing lamellar κ-carbide within γ matrix (Fig. 6(a)). Although the lamellar κ-carbide in the α matrix may contribute to the strength and ductility by secondary phase strengthening and by preventing the prolongation of microcracks that initiated from α/κ interface where strain would concentrate, the continuous distributed κ-carbide along the grain boundaries of large α grains are readily cracked and preferred to crack by cleavage fracture during tensile deformation [47]. So the ultimate strength of the specimen annealed at $7 0 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ is relatively high and the ultimate elongation is low (Fig. 16). When annealing temperature is $8 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,$ the microstructure is composed of α phase, γ phase and κ-carbide (Fig. 6(b)). Compared with the specimen annealed at $7 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ (Fig. 6(a)), the fraction of κ-carbide has decreased, and the size of κ- carbide has coarsened, which indicates that the strengthening and ductility from κ-carbide will be weakened in the specimen annealed at 800 ${ } ^ { \circ } \mathbb { C } .$ The κ-carbide still continuously distributed along the large α grains will decrease the ductility. However, the γ phase in the microstructure can increase the ductility. Additionally, at higher annealing temperature, the elimination of dislocations inherited from the hot rolling process is better developed, which normally will decrease the strength and increase the ductility. Thus, the ultimate strength of the specimen annealed at $8 0 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ decreases and the ultimate elongation increases slightly (Fig. 16). For the specimen annealed at $8 5 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,$ , the quick increase of the ductility may mainly attributed to the increase of γ phase fraction. When the annealing temperature reaches $9 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathsf { C } ,$ κ- carbide dissolves during annealing, and there is only some boundary κ-carbide of very small size formed during quenching and distributed along some large α grains (Figs. 6(d) and $1 0 ( \mathsf { a } ) ,$ . The ultimate elongation is 36.6%, and the ductility of the specimen is the best of all the annealed specimens. The ductility of the specimen annealed at $9 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathsf { C }$ would be better if no boundary κ-carbide is formed during quenching. Fig. 7(c) is the $( 2 0 0 ) _ { \mathrm { { r } } }$ κ dark-field (DF) image taken from [100]κ direction. However, not all the κ-carbide has orientation relationship with α matrix, and for example, the κ-carbide with globular shape or short-rod like shape in Fig. 7(d) shows no orientation relationship with α matrix. The coherency between κ-carbide and α phase can be destroyed during the coarsening of κ-carbide, and before annealing, some κ-carbide has already existed in the matrix and may have no orientation with the matrix. Fig. 6(b) is the SEM micrograph of the specimen annealed at $8 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathsf { C } ,$ and compared with the κ-carbide in the specimen annealed at $7 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ $\left( \mathrm { F i g . } 6 ( \mathsf { a } ) \right)$ , the size of κ-carbide is larger, the fraction of κ-carbide is lower, and the shape is closer to globular. Fig. 8(a) shows the TEM BF image of the specimen annealed at $8 0 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ and Fig. 8(b) shows the SADPs of phases in Fig. 8(a). Several areas are examined and no orientation relationship is found between κ-carbide and its neighboring α phase. Fig. 6(c) clearly shows the increase of γ phase fraction and decrease of κ-carbide fraction in the γ phase area of specimen annealed at $8 5 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ compared with the specimen annealed at $8 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathsf { C } ,$ and this trend is clearer than that shown by the comparison of OM micrographs of the two specimens (Fig. 5(b) and (d)). When the annealing temperature is between 900 °C and $1 1 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,$ , no obvious κ-carbide can be seen in the matrix (Fig. $6 ( \mathrm { d } )$ through (g)), while grain sizes of α phase and γ phase increase apparently. The average grain size change of each phase with the annealing temperature ranging from $9 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathsf { C }$ to 1200 °C, which is obtained from SEM micrographs, is shown in Fig. 9. The ferrite (marked as α ) that mainly with relatively small grain size distributed among γ grains is treated separately with the ferrite (marked as $\mathbf { \alpha } \propto _ { 2 } )$ mainly distributed continuously with banded shape and large grain size because of their large difference in average grain size. It is not difficult to understand that the contiguous α2 with banded shape is mainly the ferrite that solidified first during casting. The grain size of $\alpha _ { 2 }$ which continuously distributed and the grain size of γ phase increase quickly with the rising of annealing temperature higher than $9 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathsf { C } ,$ while the grain size of $\alpha _ { 2 }$ which is mainly distributed among γ grains does not increase quickly with the rising of annealing temperature until the temperature is over $1 0 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } .$ Additionally, the banded structure becomes less contiguous at higher temperatures (Figs. 5(a), (c), (e) and 6(h)), and this is in accordance with that in the steel Fe–20Mn–9Al–0.6C [25].

The γ phase has almost totally decomposed to α phase and κ- carbide by eutectoid reaction $\gamma \to \alpha + \kappa$ after annealing at $7 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ for 1 h, and the N–W orientation relationship is found mainly between some lamellar κ-carbide and the α matrix (Fig. 7). When annealed at $8 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ or $8 5 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,$ the γ phase volume fraction is about 29% and 39%, respectively. The κ-carbide and α phase fraction decreases, while the γ phase fraction increases with annealing temperature rising from 700 $^ \circ \mathsf { C }$ to $9 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } .$ The N–W orientation relationship has been hardly found between κ-carbide and α matrix when annealing temperature reaches $8 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } \left( \mathrm { F i g . } 8 \right)$ . In the annealing temperature range from 700 to 850 ${ } ^ { \circ } \mathbb { C } ,$ , the γ phase becomes more stable with the increase of temperature, so the γ phase fraction increases with the increase of temperature. The morphology of κ-carbide tends to be larger and closer to globular shape (Fig. 6(a) through (c)), which originates from the higher diffusion speed of elements and growth speed of κ-carbide at higher temperatures. For the sake of lowering the strain energy, the growth of κ- carbide favors to be along the plane of boundary between κ-carbide and α matrix with orientation relationship at first, and with the grow up of κ-carbide, the lattice coherency and orientation relationship will be destroyed. This is why the orientation relationship is easy to found between the lamellar κ and α matrix in the specimen annealed at 700 ${ } ^ { \circ } { \mathsf { C } } ,$ and becomes harder to be found or has disappeared when the annealing temperature rises from $7 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ to $8 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ .

When the annealing temperature is over $9 5 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,$ the grain size of both α phase and γ phase increases with the increase of temperature, and naturally, the yield strength, ultimate tensile strength and ultimate elongation will decrease with the increase of temperature. citation_sentences: Although no obvious κ-carbide is found distributed in the matrix when the annealing temperature is in the range $9 0 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ to $1 1 0 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ (Fig. 6(d) through (g)), κ-carbide is indeed found along some boundaries between large α grains in α area in the annealing temperature range $9 0 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ to $1 2 0 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ , as shown in $\mathrm { F i g . } 1 0 ( \mathsf { a } )$ through (d). TEM is used to confirm the κ-carbide along the α/α boundaries. Fig. 11(a) is the TEM BF micrograph of the specimen annealed at $1 1 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ , and the SADP of boundary κ-carbide and α matrix are shown in Fig. 11(b). Fig. 12(a) shows the TEM BF micrograph of the specimen annealed at $1 2 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ and the corresponding SADP in Fig. 12(b) shows the N–W orientation relationship between κ-carbide and α matrix. However, the (200)κ DF micrograph taken from $[ 1 1 0 ] _ { \kappa }$ direction $( \mathrm { F i g . } 1 2 ( \mathrm { c } ) )$ shows only part of the boundary κ-carbide, which indicates the large misorientation between the neighboring κ-carbide particles. The boundary κ- carbide is believed to form during the quenching when annealing temperature is over the dissolution temperature of κ-carbide, which is between $8 5 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ and $9 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathbb { C } .$ In the specimen annealed at $1 2 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,$ , there are needle shaped or plate shaped particles in some large α grains (Fig. 6(h) and Fig. 10(d)). The particle forms during the quenching, and basically has the FCC structure, and many investigations on the particles in the steels with a high Al content have been done by Hwang et al. [37–41]. EDS line scanning is done during SEM observation of the specimens annealed at $8 0 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ and $9 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathsf { C }$ to examine the elements concentrations in different phases, as shown in Fig. 13(a) and (b). In the specimen annealed at $8 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,$ Mn content is higher while Al content is lower in α phase compared with those in γ phase, and Mn and Al content in κ-carbide is the highest, higher than those in α phase or γ phase. The results are in accord with the normal conclusion well.

The volume fraction change of each phase with the temperature, which is determined from the XRD measurement results, is shown in Fig. 14. The general trend is similar with that calculated by Thermo-Calc, which is shown in Fig. 1. No γ phase is detected when annealing temperature is $7 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } _ { \scriptscriptstyle 1 }$ and no κ-carbide is detected when the annealing temperature is $9 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathbb { C } .$ As has been obtained from the OM micrographs in Fig. 5, the dissolution temperature for κ-carbide is between $8 5 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ and $9 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathsf C ,$ which is higher than that $( 8 2 3 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } )$ calculated by Thermo-Calc (Fig. 1). When the anneal temperature changes from $7 0 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ to 900 °C, the volume fractions of α phase and κ-carbide decrease, and the fraction of γ phase increases. A $t 9 0 0 ^ { \circ } C ,$ the γ phase fraction is about 60%. After the dissolution of κ-carbide, the volume fraction of γ phase first increase slightly and then decrease slightly with the increment of annealing temperature, and the fraction of γ phase reaches largest around the annealing temperature of $1 0 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } .$ The volume fraction of γ phase changes only in the small range of 57% to 63% when the annealing temperature is between $9 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathsf { C }$ and $1 2 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } .$ . The boundary κ- carbide that was seen in Fig. 10 cannot be detected by XRD, probably due to that the fraction of boundary κ-carbide is too small. In addition, TEM analysis (not shown here) indicates that no obvious superlattice spots can be seen in the SADPs of α phase or γ phase in all the annealed specimens, and although some weak superlattice spots can be seen in the SADPs of some grains; they are all too weak to obtain the DF images. So ordering of atoms in α phase or γ phase is not expected during annealing at the temperature range $7 0 0 { - } 1 2 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ for 1 h, while ordering may occur during quenching, whereas the time is too short for the ordering to well develop.

When the annealing temperature is over 900 °C, the fraction of γ phase change moderately, first increases from about 60% at $9 0 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ to about 63% at $1 0 0 0 \ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } _ { \cdot }$ and then decreases to about 57% at 1200 $^ { \circ } \ C$ (Fig. 14). The boundary κ-carbide in the specimens annealed at the temperature of or over 900 °C (Figs. 10, 11 and 12) is thought to form during quenching. With the increase of temperature, the Mn and C elements, together with Al element in the α phase with large grains segregate to the boundary and form κ-carbide. In some large α grains in the specimen annealed at $1 2 0 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,$ , the needle-like or plate-like FCC particles (Fig. 10(d)), are thought to come from the quick transformation from BCC to FCC. It is easy to know that the needle-like or plate-like particles are competing for Mn or C elements with boundary κ-carbide, since the needle-like or plate-like particles tend to disappear when becoming close to grain boundary.

TEM micrographs of the boundary κ in the specimen annealed at $1 1 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathsf C \colon ( \mathsf a )$ BF micrograph, (b) SADP of α, and κ. 1 h, the actual dissolution temperature for κ is between $8 5 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ and 900 ${ } ^ { \circ } \mathbb { C } ,$ while the dissolution temperature of κ in the equilibrium phase diagram calculated by Thermo-Calc is about 823 °C, which is lower. This may be attributed to not only the error of the thermodynamic calculation, but also the difference between equilibrium and disequilibrium system.

Among α phase, γ phase and κ-carbide in the same specimen, the Mn and Al contents in κ-carbide are the highest.
